title: Listening - Part 3: A Student Exchange
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their experience of taking part in a student exchange. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what surprised each speaker during the exchange. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their experience of taking part in a student exchange. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what surprised each speaker during the exchange. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five students talk about taking part in a student exchange.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: Before I went to Austria, I worried constantly about my German. I’d only studied it for two years, and I imagined sitting silently at meals while everybody waited for me to answer. [19]In fact, my host family spoke slowly at first, I soon learned everyday phrases, and after a few days I was joining conversations without thinking too much.[/19] Naturally I made mistakes, but people understood me. I hadn’t expected speaking another language to become comfortable so quickly.||Speaker 2 Man: I imagined our visit to Denmark would mean attending lessons in the morning and spending the rest of the time exploring with our partners. That certainly wasn’t the arrangement. [20]Every day included school, a project session, a cultural visit and then an evening activity, so there was hardly a moment when we weren’t expected to be somewhere.[/20] I enjoyed most of it, especially the music workshop, but I returned home needing a rest after what I had thought would be a fairly relaxed week.||Speaker 3 Woman: My exchange partner in Spain was friendly, and I loved the town where she lived. The biggest adjustment wasn’t the language or school; it was daily life at home. [21]Her family didn’t eat their evening meal until nearly ten, and afterwards they often went out for a walk, whereas I’m usually getting ready for bed by then.[/21] By the end I liked the relaxed evenings, although my parents laughed when I tried to continue the same routine after returning.||Speaker 4 Man: On previous school trips, teachers had always told us where to go and stayed nearby. During the exchange in the Netherlands, I expected the same kind of supervision. [22]Instead, once lessons finished, our partners showed us how to use the buses and bikes, and the adults trusted us to arrange our own afternoon plans and return in time for dinner.[/22] At first that felt strange, but it made me far more confident about finding my way around a new place.||Speaker 5 Woman: I’m usually quite shy, so before the exchange I was worried that everyone would stay with the classmates they already knew. At the welcome event, though, our teachers mixed us into small international groups to complete challenges. [23]By the end of the first afternoon, I was laughing with students from three different countries and making plans to meet them again that evening.[/23] I had expected friendships to take most of the week, not just a few hours.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: Before I went to Austria, I worried constantly about my German. I’d only studied it for two years, and I imagined sitting silently at meals while everybody waited for me to answer. In fact, my host family spoke slowly at first, I soon learned everyday phrases, and after a few days I was joining conversations without thinking too much. Naturally I made mistakes, but people understood me. I hadn’t expected speaking another language to become comfortable so quickly.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I imagined our visit to Denmark would mean attending lessons in the morning and spending the rest of the time exploring with our partners. That certainly wasn’t the arrangement. Every day included school, a project session, a cultural visit and then an evening activity, so there was hardly a moment when we weren’t expected to be somewhere. I enjoyed most of it, especially the music workshop, but I returned home needing a rest after what I had thought would be a fairly relaxed week.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My exchange partner in Spain was friendly, and I loved the town where she lived. The biggest adjustment wasn’t the language or school; it was daily life at home. Her family didn’t eat their evening meal until nearly ten, and afterwards they often went out for a walk, whereas I’m usually getting ready for bed by then. By the end I liked the relaxed evenings, although my parents laughed when I tried to continue the same routine after returning.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: On previous school trips, teachers had always told us where to go and stayed nearby. During the exchange in the Netherlands, I expected the same kind of supervision. Instead, once lessons finished, our partners showed us how to use the buses and bikes, and the adults trusted us to arrange our own afternoon plans and return in time for dinner. At first that felt strange, but it made me far more confident about finding my way around a new place.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I’m usually quite shy, so before the exchange I was worried that everyone would stay with the classmates they already knew. At the welcome event, though, our teachers mixed us into small international groups to complete challenges. By the end of the first afternoon, I was laughing with students from three different countries and making plans to meet them again that evening. I had expected friendships to take most of the week, not just a few hours.

options.text: how much independence they were given

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker expected major language difficulties but soon communicated comfortably (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker was surprised by the large number of scheduled activities (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker noticed major differences in the host family's daily schedule (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker did not expect to be trusted to arrange activities and travel around independently (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker was surprised that social relationships developed very quickly (evidence [23]).
